# G) Glossary

---
Slide G01 — Core metrics  
1) Purpose – Give plain definitions with units.  
2) What investors expect – Short, unambiguous lines.  
3) Gather this first – Your own windows.  
4) Build it in your slide tool – Title 48 pt. Two-column list, 24–26 pt.  
5) Copy-ready text – Title: “Core metrics.” Body: “Use these terms consistently across slides and exports.”  
6) Figure – [Figure: Glossary list]  
TAM – Everyone who could buy games like yours.  
SAM – The part you can reach given platform and geo.  
SOM – The realistic slice you plan to capture.  
D1, D7, D30 – Retention rates on day 1, 7, and 30.  
ARPDAU – Money per DAU per day.  
ARPPU – Money per payer over a period.  
7) Callouts – [Pro tip] Put windows next to each metric where used.  
8) QA checks – Units present; brevity; no overlap.  
9) Common mistakes – Changing definitions mid-deck.  
10) Footnote – [Footnote: Common industry definitions, 2025-11-17]

---
Slide G02 — UA and attribution  
1) Purpose – Clarify UA and attribution terms.  
2) What investors expect – Clean, current definitions.  
3) Gather this first – Your current attribution settings.  
4) Build it in your slide tool – Title 48 pt. Two-column list.  
5) Copy-ready text – Title: “UA and attribution.” Body: “Declare windows and methods wherever you show cohorts.”  
6) Figure – [Figure: Glossary list]  
LTV – Lifetime value per user over a chosen window.  
CAC – Cost to acquire a customer.  
CPI – Cost per install.  
ROAS – Return on ad spend.  
AdAttributionKit – Apple’s framework for privacy-safe attribution.  
Android Privacy Sandbox – Google’s attribution approach without device identifiers.  
7) Callouts – [Critical to consider] Align LTV and CAC windows.  
8) QA checks – Windows clear; acronyms expanded.  
9) Common mistakes – Silent windows; mixing frameworks.  
10) Footnote – [Footnote: Attribution frameworks, 2025-11-17]

---
Slide G03 — Product and operations  
1) Purpose – Clarify internal delivery terms.  
2) What investors expect – Shared meanings across the team.  
3) Gather this first – Your pipeline steps.  
4) Build it in your slide tool – Title 48 pt. Two-column list.  
5) Copy-ready text – Title: “Product and ops.” Body: “Use consistent names across PRDs, decks, and logs.”  
6) Figure – [Figure: Glossary list]  
FTUE – First time user experience.  
Cohort – Users starting in the same window by platform and country.  
Live-ops – Planned content and events shipped on a cadence.  
Run rate – Monthly or annualised extrapolation of current revenue.  
Payback – Months required for net margin per user to cover CAC.  
7) Callouts – [Pro tip] Put examples in your internal wiki.  
8) QA checks – Terms unique; lines short.  
9) Common mistakes – Multiple names for the same step.  
10) Footnote – [Footnote: Internal glossary template, 2025-11-17]

# H) Final checklists and rubrics

---
Slide H01 — Final one-minute skim  
1) Purpose – Provide a last pass before you send.  
2) What investors expect – A deck with one idea per slide and readable figures.  
3) Gather this first – Latest numbers and links.  
4) Build it in your slide tool – Title 48 pt. Short checklist, 26–28 pt.  
5) Copy-ready text – Title: “Final skim.” Body: “One clear claim per slide. One primary visual. Dates on every number. Dual currency on money. Methods in captions. Readable at 100 percent zoom.”  
6) Figure – [Figure: Checklist]  
7) Callouts – [Pro tip] Export to PDF and check on a laptop at 100 percent.  
8) QA checks – Links work; fonts consistent; contrast high; typos fixed.  
9) Common mistakes – Overstuffed slides; missing dates; blended cohorts.  
10) Footnote – [Footnote: Internal delivery checklist, 2025-11-17]

---
Slide H02 — Acceptance rubric 0 to 5  
1) Purpose – Define what “good” looks like.  
2) What investors expect – A deck that earns a 4 or 5 on clarity and credibility.  
3) Gather this first – Your best version and one peer reviewer.  
4) Build it in your slide tool – Title 48 pt. Small table of criteria.  
5) Copy-ready text – Title: “What earns a 5.” Body: “A 5 is clear, complete, and verifiable. All numbers dated and methods stated.”  
6) Figure – [Figure: Rubric]  

| Score | Description |  
|---|---|  
| 0–1 | Unclear or missing basics |  
| 2–3 | Some structure, gaps remain |  
| 4 | Clear, minor edits needed |  
| 5 | Clear, complete, verifiable |  

7) Callouts – [Pro tip] Have the reviewer initial the slide.  
8) QA checks – Criteria match the guide; reviewer named.  
9) Common mistakes – Vague scoring; no reviewer.  
10) Footnote – [Footnote: Review rubric, 2025-11-17]

---
Slide H03 — Versioning and change log  
1) Purpose – Track what changed between sends.  
2) What investors expect – A tidy log with dates and reasons.  
3) Gather this first – Version tag; change notes.  
4) Build it in your slide tool – Title 48 pt. One small table with three rows.  
5) Copy-ready text – Title: “Change log.” Body: “Tag each send and note what changed and why.”  
6) Figure – [Figure: Change log table]  

| Version | Date | What changed and why |  
|---|---|---|  
| v1.0 | 2025-11-17 | First complete draft |  
| v1.1 | 2025-11-20 | Updated KPIs and gates |  
| v1.2 | 2025-12-01 | Refined ask and tranches |  

7) Callouts – [Pro tip] Keep the log to one slide.  
8) QA checks – Dates present; changes concise; reasons plain.  
9) Common mistakes – No log; vague notes.  
10) Footnote – [Footnote: Versioning practice, 2025-11-17]

# C) Series A deck tutorial

---
Slide C01 — Title with current state  
1) Purpose – Open with facts: installs, MAU, net revenue run rate.  
2) What investors expect – A concise status line with dates and platforms.  
3) Gather this first – Last 30 days installs, MAU, net revenue; date range; platforms.  
4) Build it in your slide tool – Title 48–56 pt. One 28 pt status line. Place a small facts table beneath. Left align.  
5) Copy-ready text – Title: “Live, growing, and disciplined.” Body: “30-day installs 120,000. MAU 85,000. Net revenue £95,000 | $118,750. 2025-09-15 to 2025-10-14.”  
6) Figure – [Figure: Status table]

| Metric | 30-day value |
|---|---:|
| Installs | 120,000 |
| MAU | 85,000 |
| Net revenue | £95,000 | $118,750 |

7) Callouts – [Pro tip] Keep the time window exact.  
8) QA checks – Dates visible; net not gross; platforms noted elsewhere; dual currency.  
9) Common mistakes – Undated numbers; blended platforms.  
10) Footnote – [Footnote: Illustrative values, 2025-10, method: internal analytics export]

Slide C02 — Progress since Seed: experiment wins and kills

4) Build it in your slide tool – Title 48 pt. Two-column table: Wins and Kills.

| Wins | Result | Why |
|---|---:|---|
| Hook edit | +4% D1 | Clearer early reward |
| Creator batch 2 | D7 ROAS 23% | Better brief |
| Store A/B | +6% icon CTR | Cleaner shape |

| Kills | Result | Why |
|---|---:|---|
| Early price up | −8% conversion | Value mismatch |
| Long match mode | −3% D1 | Slow sessions |

7) Callouts – [Critical to consider] Kill quickly when gates are missed.  
8) QA checks – Each row has a number; reasons short; dates captured in notes.  
9) Common mistakes – No kills; vague wins.  
10) Footnote – [Footnote: Sample test log excerpt, 2025-11-17]

# D) Due Diligence overview

---
Slide D01 — Diligence: Executive summary and index  
1) Purpose – Give investors a single page that tells them what is here and where to click.  
2) What investors expect – Short summary, live links, owners, and last refresh dates.  
3) Gather this first – Folder links for Legal, Product, Data, Finance, UA, Tech, Compliance, Risks; document owners; refresh dates.  
4) Build it in your slide tool – Title 48 pt. Two-column table 24–26 pt: Package, Link, Owner, Last updated. Add one sentence at top that says how to read the pack.  
5) Copy-ready text – Title: “How to read this diligence pack.” Body: “Open the section you need from the table. Each link goes to a folder with dated exports and a short ‘open this first’ note.”  
6) Figure – [Figure: Diligence index]

| Package | Link | Owner | Updated |
|---|---|---|---|
| Legal | [link] | COO | 2025-11-01 |
| Product | [link] | CPO | 2025-11-12 |
| Data | [link] | Head of Data | 2025-11-14 |
| Finance | [link] | CFO | 2025-11-10 |

7) Callouts – [Pro tip] Put a README.txt in every folder with a one-paragraph guide.  
8) QA checks – All links work; dates recent; owners named; file names human readable.  
9) Common mistakes – Dead links; mixed naming; no refresh dates.  
10) Footnote – [Footnote: Packaging pattern used in standard venture processes, 2025-11-17]

---
Slide D02 — Diligence: Legal and company structure  
1) Purpose – Show the legal entities, ownership, and key agreements at a glance.  
2) What investors expect – Diagram of entities, jurisdiction, and a short table of key contracts with status.  
3) Gather this first – Certificate of incorporation, shareholder agreement, IP assignment agreements, publisher or platform agreements.  
4) Build it in your slide tool – Title 48 pt. Left: simple org chart of entities. Right: contracts table.  
5) Copy-ready text – Title: “Company structure and key agreements.” Body: “Single operating company with IP held in the same entity. All staff and contractors have signed IP assignments.”  
6) Figure – [Figure: Entity diagram and contract list]

| Contract | Counterparty | Scope | Status |
|---|---|---|---|
| Employment IP assignment | All staff | Work-made-for-hire | Signed |
| Contractor IP | Named vendors | Code, art | Signed |
| Store agreements | Apple, Google, Steam | Distribution | Active |

7) Callouts – [Critical to consider] If IP is in a different entity, say why and link the assignment.  
8) QA checks – Jurisdictions listed; signatures confirmed; renewal dates shown.  
9) Common mistakes – Missing contractor assignments; outdated store agreements.  
10) Footnote – [Footnote: Legal snapshot, 2025-11-12, method: counsel summary]

Slide D03 — Diligence: Cap table and options  
1) Purpose – Provide the fully diluted ownership view.  
2) What investors expect – Table with ordinary shares, preferred, options, and reserved pool.  
3) Gather this first – Latest cap table spreadsheet; board-approved option pool size; vesting schedules.  
4) Build it in your slide tool – Title 48 pt. One clean table. Add a one-line note on vesting standard.  
5) Copy-ready text – Title: “Fully diluted ownership.” Body: “Option pool 12 percent post-money target, four-year vesting with one-year cliff.”  
6) Figure – [Figure: Cap table]

| Holder | Class | % FD | Notes |
|---|---|---:|---|
| Founders | Ordinary | 54% | 4-year vesting |
| Seed investors | Preferred | 24% | 1x non-participating |
| ESOP granted | Options | 6% | Standard terms |
| ESOP ungranted | Options | 6% | Pool |
| Misc. | Ordinary | 10% | Advisors etc. |

7) Callouts – [Pro tip] Show liquidation preference summary in notes.  
8) QA checks – Totals equal 100 percent; terms stated plainly; dates current.  
9) Common mistakes – Hiding granted vs ungranted; unclear preferences.  
10) Footnote – [Footnote: Cap table excerpt, 2025-11-10, method: FD view from counsel file]

| Week | Event | Release |
|---|---|---|

Pipeline: CI build → QA gate → Flagged rollout → Monitor → Rollback plan.

|---|---|---|

| Field | Type | Notes |
|---|---|---|

|---|---|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| Oct | iOS | 34% | 10% | 4% | £0.61 | $0.76 |
| Oct | Android | 31% | 9% | 3% | £0.49 | $0.61 |

7) Callouts – [Critical to consider] Never blend platforms or months.

Slide D09 — Diligence: Attribution method per platform  
1) Purpose – Explain how installs and revenue are attributed.  
2) What investors expect – Clear method notes for iOS, Android, Steam, and Telegram if used.  
3) Gather this first – SKAN or AdAttributionKit settings, Android Privacy Sandbox status, Steam referral tagging, Telegram parameters.  
4) Build it in your slide tool – Title 48 pt. Platform table with Method, Window, Notes.  
5) Copy-ready text – Title: “Attribution methods.” Body: “We keep channel windows separate and align payback with the same window.”  
6) Figure – [Figure: Attribution table]

| Platform | Method | Window | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| iOS | AdAttributionKit | 24–35 days | Lockback by campaign |
| Android | Privacy Sandbox | 7–30 days | SAN support |
| Steam | UTM + store data | 30 days | Referral tags |
| Telegram | Deep links | Session-based | Bot parameters |

7) Callouts – [Pro tip] Show which LTV window matches each method.  
8) QA checks – Methods per platform; windows stated; notes brief.  
9) Common mistakes – One-size windows; mixing SKAN with MMP data.  
10) Footnote – [Footnote: Attribution setup summary, 2025-11-15]

|---|---|---|

# E) Tutorials and worked examples

---
Slide E01 — Tutorial: LTV and payback step-by-step  
1) Purpose – Teach how to compute LTV and payback with a tiny example.  
2) What investors expect – Clear definitions and one worked calculation.  
3) Gather this first – ARPDAU, active days per user in 90-day window, refunds percent, CAC.  
4) Build it in your slide tool – Title 48 pt. One table with a worked calculation.  
5) Copy-ready text – Title: “Compute LTV and payback.” Body: “LTV = ARPDAU × active days × (1 − refund rate). Payback compares LTV to CAC.”  
6) Figure – [Figure: Worked example]

| Item | Value |
|---|---|
| ARPDAU | £0.06 | $0.08 |
| Active days (D90) | 10.0 |
| Refunds | 1.3% |
| LTV | £0.59 | $0.74 |
| CAC | £0.56 | $0.70 |
| Payback | 9 months |

7) Callouts – [Critical to consider] Always state the LTV window.  
8) QA checks – Maths correct; currency dual; window labelled.  
9) Common mistakes – Ignoring refunds; mixing windows.  
10) Footnote – [Footnote: Example only, 2025-11-17]

---
Slide E02 — Tutorial: Build a cohort chart  
1) Purpose – Show how to turn a cohort table into a simple retention chart.  
2) What investors expect – Clean axes, readable labels, no blends.  
3) Gather this first – Weekly retention percentages for two cohorts.  
4) Build it in your slide tool – Title 48 pt. Insert a line chart; x-axis Weeks 0–8; y-axis Percent.  
5) Copy-ready text – Title: “From table to chart.” Body: “Keep lines to two cohorts max on the slide; move the rest to an appendix.”  
6) Figure – [Figure: Chart spec] X-axis Weeks; Y-axis Retention percent. Two lines: iOS and Android.  
7) Callouts – [Pro tip] Label lines directly rather than using a legend if your tool allows.  
8) QA checks – Fonts legible at 100 percent zoom; axes labelled; dates in caption.  
9) Common mistakes – Six lines; axis without units.  
10) Footnote – [Footnote: Charting pattern, 2025-11-17]

---
Slide E03 — Tutorial: EU fee margin bridge  
1) Purpose – Teach a simple gross to net calculation.  
2) What investors expect – A small waterfall that matches finance numbers.  
3) Gather this first – Store fee percent, refunds, VAT treatment if applicable.  
4) Build it in your slide tool – Title 48 pt. Four-row bridge.  
5) Copy-ready text – Title: “From gross to net.” Body: “Make fees visible so readers trust your net numbers.”  
6) Figure – [Figure: Bridge]

| Step | £ | $ |
|---|---:|---:|
| Gross revenue | £100,000 | $125,000 |
| Store fees 30% | −£30,000 | −$37,500 |
| Refunds 1.3% | −£1,300 | −$1,625 |
| Net | £68,700 | $85,875 |

7) Callouts – [Caution] Clarify VAT inclusion in gross.  
8) QA checks – Maths reconciles; dual currency; labels clear.  
9) Common mistakes – Hiding refunds; mixing net and gross on the same slide.  
10) Footnote – [Footnote: Worked example, 2025-11-17]

---
Slide E04 — Tutorial: Experiment design template  
1) Purpose – Provide a one-pager template for any test.  
2) What investors expect – Hypothesis, metric, sample size guardrail, stop rule, owner, date.  
3) Gather this first – Hypothesis statement, gate metric, data source, dates.  
4) Build it in your slide tool – Title 48 pt. Simple form table.  
5) Copy-ready text – Title: “Design every test the same way.” Body: “Short, predictable templates make execution faster and review easier.”  
6) Figure – [Figure: Test template]

| Field | Example |
|---|---|
| Hypothesis | New hook lifts D1 by 3% |
| Metric | D1 retention |
| Sample guardrail | 2,000 installs |
| Stop rule | Stop if D1 falls 2% |
| Owner | UA lead |
| Window | 7 days |

7) Callouts – [Pro tip] Keep your test library searchable by tag.  
8) QA checks – All fields filled; dates present; metric source stated.  
9) Common mistakes – Vague hypotheses; no stop rule.  
10) Footnote – [Footnote: Template used across teams, 2025-11-17]

|---|---:|---:|---|

# G) Glossary

---
Slide G01 — Glossary A–M  
1) Purpose – Define core terms in plain English.  
2) What investors expect – Short, accurate definitions.  
3) Gather this first – Terms used in your deck.  
4) Build it in your slide tool – Title 48 pt. Two-column list.  
5) Copy-ready text – Title: “Glossary A–M.” Body: “Keep the same definitions in your data notes.”  
6) Figure – [Figure: Terms]

TAM – Total addressable market.  
SAM – Serviceable available market.  
SOM – Serviceable obtainable market.  
D1, D7, D30 – Retention at day 1, 7, 30.  
ARPDAU – Average revenue per daily active user.  
ARPPU – Average revenue per paying user.  
LTV – Lifetime value over a stated window.  
CAC – Customer acquisition cost.  
Payback – Time for LTV to exceed CAC.

7) Callouts – [Pro tip] Add your own internal terms here.  
8) QA checks – Short; accurate; no overlap.  
9) Common mistakes – Confusing ARPDAU with ARPPU.  
10) Footnote – [Footnote: Terms used throughout, 2025-11-17]

---
Slide G02 — Glossary N–Z  
1) Purpose – Continue definitions.  
2) What investors expect – Clear, platform-aware terms.  
3) Gather this first – UA and privacy terms.  
4) Build it in your slide tool – Title 48 pt. Two-column list.  
5) Copy-ready text – Title: “Glossary N–Z.” Body: “Match these in your appendix evidence table.”  
6) Figure – [Figure: Terms]

FTUE – First-time user experience.  
UA – User acquisition.  
CPI – Cost per install.  
ROAS – Return on ad spend.  
SKAN or AdAttributionKit – iOS attribution frameworks.  
Android Privacy Sandbox – Android privacy-focused attribution.  
Refund rate – Percent of purchases refunded.  
Contribution – Net revenue minus variable costs.

7) Callouts – [Caution] Do not invent new KPI names.  
8) QA checks – Definitions match methods; kept short.  
9) Common mistakes – Outdated attribution terms.  
10) Footnote – [Footnote: Terms used throughout, 2025-11-17]

---
Slide G03 — Glossary: Roles and documents  
1) Purpose – Define the roles and core documents mentioned.  
2) What investors expect – Clarity on who does what.  
3) Gather this first – Your team’s role names.  
4) Build it in your slide tool – Title 48 pt. Two-column list.  
5) Copy-ready text – Title: “Roles and documents.” Body: “Map your team to these roles in your Team slide.”  
6) Figure – [Figure: List]

CPO – Head of product design and economy.  
UA lead – Runs channels and tests.  
Producer – Schedules, live-ops cadence.  
GDD – Game design document.  
TDD – Technical design document.  
README – One-paragraph guide in every folder.

7) Callouts – [Pro tip] Keep role coverage visible in the Team slide.  
8) QA checks – Titles match your team; concise.  
9) Common mistakes – Inflated titles for contractors.  
10) Footnote – [Footnote: Role naming, 2025-11-17]

# H) Final checklists and rubrics

---
Slide H01 — Final check: One-minute skim  
1) Purpose – Ensure a busy investor can grasp the story in one minute.  
2) What investors expect – Clear titles, one visual per slide, and obvious takeaways.  
3) Gather this first – Your latest draft.  
4) Build it in your slide tool – Title 48 pt. Checklist 26 pt.  
5) Copy-ready text – Title: “One-minute skim.” Body: “Titles tell the story, figures are labelled, and dates are visible.”  
6) Figure – [Figure: Checklist]

Checklist: titles tell the story; each slide has one visual; axes labelled and units shown; dates present; methods in captions; links tested.  
7) Callouts – [Pro tip] Ask a peer to skim in silence and retell the story.  
8) QA checks – Skim passes; no dense walls of text; consistent fonts.  
9) Common mistakes – Two visuals fighting; unlabeled axes.  
10) Footnote – [Footnote: Skim test method, 2025-11-17]

---
Slide H02 — Final check: Readability and completeness  
1) Purpose – Catch legibility and missing pieces before sending.  
2) What investors expect – Accessible text, consistent currency, and no missing links.  
3) Gather this first – Accessibility checker results; currency passes; link tests.  
4) Build it in your slide tool – Title 48 pt. Two-column pass list.  
5) Copy-ready text – Title: “Readable and complete.” Body: “Fonts are large, colours high contrast, and every figure has a caption with method and date.”  
6) Figure – [Figure: Pass list]

Pass: Title ≥ 40 pt; body ≥ 24 pt; labels ≥ 16 pt; contrast meets WCAG AA; GBP and USD shown side by side where money appears; appendix index present.  
7) Callouts – [Caution] Colour-blind safe palettes help reviewers.  
8) QA checks – All passes checked; dates on every metric.  
9) Common mistakes – Tiny labels; missing captions.  
10) Footnote – [Footnote: Accessibility standards summary, 2025-11-17]

---
Slide H03 — Acceptance rubric and versioning  
1) Purpose – Define what “good” looks like and how to version the deck.  
2) What investors expect – A visible bar and a simple versioning rule.  
3) Gather this first – Your team’s acceptance bar and file naming pattern.  
4) Build it in your slide tool – Title 48 pt. Table with Score, What it means, Example fixes.  
5) Copy-ready text – Title: “Acceptance rubric and versioning.” Body: “Ship at 4 or 5 only. Use semantic versioning and a change log.”  
6) Figure – [Figure: Rubric]

| Score | Meaning | Example fix |
|---|---|---|
| 3 | Understandable but rough | Raise font sizes, add dates |
| 4 | Clear and convincing | Minor polish only |
| 5 | Ready for investors | No fixes found |

Version rule: DeckName_YYYYMMDD_v1.2.pptx. Maintain a one-line change log at the end.  
7) Callouts – [Pro tip] Lock the template and track deltas.  
8) QA checks – Score 4 or 5 before sending; version tag updated; change log present.  
9) Common mistakes – Sending at score 3; no change log.  
10) Footnote – [Footnote: Rubric pattern and versioning rule, 2025-11-17]
